item.title: I replaced NotebookLM with this free tool that uses my local LLMs
item.itunes:title: I replaced NotebookLM with this free tool that uses my local LLMs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Although Claude Code has been all the rage as of late, there are a bunch of other AI-powered tools that deserve to be in the spotlight. Take NotebookLM, for instance. It’s capable of analyzing your notes, blog links, videos, and presentation slides, and can generate precise information from cited sources, making it far better than chatbots for research and technical documentation.

Pair it with an array of features under its belt and Google’s generous free plan, and even a self-hosting enthusiast like me has to admit that it’s a productivity-enhancing behemoth of an app. That said, I’ve spent the last couple of months building entirely local LLM pipelines using old hardware, so I wanted to see if I could find something that could replace NotebookLM and QoL-enhancing tools. And after stumbling upon Open Notebook and using it for a while, it has replaced Google’s offering for my research tasks.

I don't 'Watch' YouTube videos anymore, I consume them using NotebookLM

NotebookLM just changed the way I YouTube.

15
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Unlike NotebookLM, Open Notebook doesn’t restrict me to Google’s models

I’ve paired it with my local LLMs and AI models

As much as I appreciate NotebookLM, it’s not really something I prefer to use for my research tasks. Call me a self-hosted addict if you will, but I’ve grown averse to privacy-intrusive cloud platforms. So, storing my study material, notes, and work-related documentation on an external company’s servers makes little sense. Not to mention, a lot of the sources I aggregate on my note takers are official documents on experimental devices, beta packages, or early access distros – undisclosed products that I absolutely can’t upload to a cloud platform. Combine the random notes containing details of my home lab (especially errors and how I mitigated them) I use for documenting my tinkering hub, and you can see why Open Notebook just wouldn’t cut it for my needs.

That’s where Open Notebook comes in handy. Rather than forcing me to upload everything on Google’s servers, I can just point it to an OpenAI-compatible endpoint – like my llama-server instances (yeah, I’ve got more than one PC running llama.cpp) – and have Open Notebook harness my local LLMs for all embedding, analysis, inference, and RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) tasks. And let me tell you, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B is an absolute beast at every Open Notebook task I throw at it, especially with nomic-embed-text handling the embedding tasks. But if that’s not really your thing, you can even pair Open Notebook with other cloud providers, making it a solid alternative for folks who want to break away from Google’s ecosystem.

Open Notebook includes plenty of analysis tools

I rely a lot on RAG search and simple summary generator

Just like NotebookLM, Open Notebook can ingest everything from conventional Office documents to video recordings. These serve as the sources for “notebooks,” which are essentially file aggregation units where LLMs can go through my documents and notes to answer my queries. That way, I can look for a specific piece of information without perusing dozens of files. Since my LLMs process everything based on the source documents and cite all the references, I don’t have to worry about hallucinated clanker slop tarnishing my research. Open Notebook also lets me save chat snippets as Markdown notes, so I don’t lose the AI-highlighted information once it gets buried under walls of queries.

Aside from the notebooks, the app also lets me run analysis tasks (called insights) on the source documents themselves, and there are quite a few of these. The simple summary and key insights operations work well for typical blog posts and Office files, while paper analysis provides better results for complex documents involving a lot of technical jargon. The reflection questions can generate quizzes, though I must admit that I never use them for my research and documentation work. Open Notebook lets me fine-tune the prompts for each insight operation, and I can even use the Playground facility to test the results before I go about creating new transformation tasks.

There’s also a general search facility that queries the entire knowledge base, but I prefer using dedicated notebooks for prompting my LLMs, as it’s faster and ensures the outputs aren’t all over the place.

4 features in NotebookLM that changed how I study

At this point, I might just owe NotebookLM my degree.

5
By  Mahnoor Faisal

It can even turn regular notes into podcasts

But it’s a bit of a pain to set up

The podcast facility is one of the game-changing features of NotebookLM, as it turns boring documents into podcasts involving two speakers. Well, Open Notebook bumps the max number of speakers to four, but I can also opt for solo narrations for an audiobook-like experience. Here’s the funny part: the main documentation page on its GitHub app lists that the text-to-speech facility isn’t available for Ollama or OpenAI-compatible (not to be confused with native OpenAI) models, but it’s entirely possible to connect TTS tools to Open Notebook via the latter – and it’s something I only realized after digging deep into the project’s .md files.

Anyway, I used a containerized version of Speaches for text-to-speech (and also speech-to-text, in case of audio transcriptions) tasks, and used the OpenAI-compatible option within the Models tab to hook it up to my Open Notebook instance. However, I had to modify the episode and speaker profiles to match my local LLMs and TTS models, which was a bit cumbersome to configure the first time. I’ve mostly created 15–17-minute podcasts containing academic materials for me to listen to during mind-numbing chores, and each generation task took around 20-ish minutes. The audio quality is also surprisingly good even on barebones speaker profiles, and I also appreciate that I could give the speakers backgrounds and descriptions to better my narration needs.

Open Notebook is a fantastic tool for research

Once I got the podcast facility up and running, Open Notebook quickly shot to the top of my collection of self-hosted LLM-powered services. After all, it provides the same notebook-esque tools as its Google counterparts and includes solid AI insight provisions. Best of all, it supports local LLMs, so my private research, notes, and documents don’t leave my local network.

Open Notebook
See at Github See at Official Site

item.title: xTool's M2 does color printing, laser cutting, and engraving for $599—and it actually changes the game for makers
item.itunes:title: xTool's M2 does color printing, laser cutting, and engraving for $599—and it actually changes the game for makers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
xTool recently announced the M2 Color Craft Laser, a new desktop crafting machine that starts at $599 and combines laser engraving, laser cutting, and CMYK color printing in one enclosed unit. Considering that desktop laser machines with beginner-friendly software, camera alignment, and enclosed safety designs usually move out of casual-crafter territory quickly, it’s encouraging to see a price under $600. The M2 is clearly aimed at the person who has outgrown a vinyl cutter or basic craft printer, but doesn’t want to turn a spare room into a full production shop.

Engraved texture, cut shapes, printed color, and smart alignment are what make the M2 feel different from a basic craft cutter.

The pitch is simple, but the device itself is more interesting. xTool isn’t just selling another diode laser with a new shell and friendlier marketing. The M2 adds a CMYK inkjet module, dual cameras for visual positioning, automated material-handling features, and swappable tool modules that let it switch between printing, cutting, engraving, and IR marking. That makes it feel less like a single-purpose laser engraver and more like a compact craft workstation, especially for creators who make personalized goods, stickers, leather accessories, coasters, signs, tags, and small merch runs.

Disclaimer: xTool provided me with the M2, equipped with a 10W diode laser module and a CMYK printhead, but had no input on this article.

8 unique 3D prints that I actually use every day

These are the genuinely useful 3D prints that I use the most in my home.

2
By  Adam Conway

The big feature is color without leaving the machine

Print and cut makes the M2 feel different

The headline feature is the M2’s color print-and-cut workflow. xTool says the CMYK inkjet module can print directly on materials such as wood, paper, felt, and similar porous surfaces, then use the laser to cut or engrave the same project without moving it to another machine. That is the part that could make the M2 click with everyday creators. Anyone who has bounced between a printer, cutting machine, transfer process, and alignment jig knows how quickly a simple project turns into a desk full of almost-right results.

The M2’s color printing is not the same thing as UV printing. It is best suited for porous or coated materials where the ink can bond properly, while smooth surfaces like glass, metal, or ceramic may need extra prep. That does not make the feature less useful, but it does mean buyers should think of it as a craft-focused print-and-cut system rather than a universal color-on-anything machine.

That workflow also gives the M2 a more approachable identity than many desktop lasers. Laser engraving can be intimidating because the best results often depend on material tests, alignment tricks, masking, ventilation, and a pile of settings that don’t mean much to a beginner. Adding color printing gives users something more familiar to start with. A printed sticker, tag, or small sign is easier to understand than dialing in engraving depth on an unfamiliar blank.

Credit: Source: xTool

This is where xTool’s camera system matters. The M2 uses dual cameras to provide a real-time view of the work area, with automated positioning and material detection handled through its ACS system. In plain terms, the machine is designed to show users where the design will land before they commit to the job. That won’t remove the need for testing, especially with laser work, but it does lower the number of ways a beginner can ruin material before learning anything useful.

The price makes this a much bigger announcement

This undercuts expectations for color-crafting hardware

The $599 starting price is the most aggressive part of the announcement. xTool already sells more advanced machines, including the M1 Ultra and higher-end laser systems, so the M2 is not trying to replace the company’s full lineup. It feels more like an attempt to bring the most approachable parts of those machines into a lower-cost package. That’s a smart move because the casual craft market is full of people who want better results than a blade cutter can offer, but don’t want to start with a four-figure machine.

There are limits baked into that price, of course. The base configuration starts with a 10W diode module, while xTool also lists support for a 20W diode module, a 3W IR module, and an inkjet module, depending on the configuration. That means buyers will need to pay close attention to bundles before assuming every feature shown in promotional material comes in the cheapest box. The M2’s flexibility is a real selling point, but modular systems always carry the risk that the most exciting workflows require additional spending.

Even with that caveat, the entry price changes the conversation. A $599 enclosed machine with color printing, camera preview, and laser capability is positioned far closer to mainstream craft hardware than to traditional workshop gear. It also gives xTool a different answer to users who might otherwise compare a laser cutter against a Cricut-style cutting machine, a sublimation setup, or a small UV printer. The M2 doesn’t beat all of those devices at everything, but it could be easier to justify as a first serious maker tool.

Real projects are where the M2 starts making sense

Leather, stone, and print-and-cut show its range

The M2’s spec sheet is useful, but the machine becomes much easier to understand once it starts producing finished objects. Engraving PU leather shows the softer, more personal side of what a compact laser can do, especially for small accessories, tags, patches, and custom gifts. Rock coasters push it in a different direction, giving the laser a harder surface where contrast, positioning, and repeatability matter more. Those two examples alone make the M2 feel less like a novelty craft machine and more like a practical desktop tool.

The print-and-cut workflow adds the missing piece. Instead of treating color as a separate step handled by another device, the M2 can print a design and then cut or engrave around it in the same workspace. That matters for stickers, labels, ornaments, tags, and small merch projects where alignment is usually the part that ruins the mood. When it works well, the process feels less fussy than juggling a printer, cutter, transfer sheet, and a pile of measurements.

That variety is the M2’s strongest argument. It is not just a laser engraver with color tacked onto the side, and it is not just a craft printer with a cutter attached. It sits somewhere between those categories, making it more flexible than a basic cutting machine but less intimidating than a full-workshop laser. The best use cases are the ones that combine its strengths: engraved texture, cut shapes, printed color, and materials that make sense for the workflow.

This could become the beginner laser to watch

The M2 matters because it reduces friction

Credit: Source: xTool

The xTool M2 isn’t the most powerful machine in xTool’s lineup, and it isn’t trying to be. Its real appeal is that it packages several maker workflows into a safer, more approachable desktop device at a price that does not immediately push beginners away. The enclosed design, camera positioning, automated setup tools, and swappable modules all point toward the same goal. xTool wants the M2 to feel less like shop equipment and more like a household creative tool.

Deals

Save on Maker & Creator Deals for Laser, Print, and Craft Gear

Explore discounts on maker and creator deals — shop savings on desktop laser cutters, print-and-cut workstations, CMYK inks, replacement modules, and air filtration units. Compare offers and bundles to find the best value for your next craft setup.
Deals Explore Maker & Creator Deals

That does not mean buyers should ignore the fine print. The most affordable bundle may not include every module, color printing works best on specific materials, and serious laser use still requires ventilation planning. An optional air purifier is available, and xTool recommends air filtration for indoor laser workflows. Those are normal tradeoffs, but they are still tradeoffs worth making clear.

Even so, the M2 looks like one of xTool’s most accessible launches yet. The combination of color printing, laser cutting, engraving, and camera-assisted setup gives it a broader creative range than a basic craft cutter, while the $599 starting price keeps it from feeling locked behind pro-maker pricing. For users who want to personalize gifts, make small batches of merch, experiment with leather and coasters, or finally try print-and-cut projects without juggling multiple devices, this could be the xTool machine that makes the category feel less intimidating.

xTool M2 Color Craft Laser
$549 $599 Save $50
Brand
xTool
Laser Power
10W diode base, 20W diode and 3W IR available
Working Area
16.7in x 12.5in (424mm x 317mm
Software
xTool Studio
Connectivity
USB/Wi-Fi
$549 at xTool

That means buyers will need to pay close attention to bundles before assuming every feature shown in promotional material comes in the cheapest box. The M2’s flexibility is a real selling point, but modular systems always carry the risk that the most exciting workflows require additional spending.

Even with that caveat, the entry price changes the conversation. A $599 enclosed machine with color printing, camera preview, and laser capability is positioned far closer to mainstream craft hardware than to traditional workshop gear. It also gives xTool a different answer to users who might otherwise compare a laser cutter against a Cricut-style cutting machine, a sublimation setup, or a small UV printer. The M2 doesn’t beat all of those devices at everything, but it could be easier to justify as a first serious maker tool.

Real projects are where the M2 starts making sense

Leather, stone, and print-and-cut show its range

The M2’s spec sheet is useful, but the machine becomes much easier to understand once it starts producing finished objects. Engraving PU leather shows the softer, more personal side of what a compact laser can do, especially for small accessories, tags, patches, and custom gifts. Rock coasters push it in a different direction, giving the laser a harder surface where contrast, positioning, and repeatability matter more. Those two examples alone make the M2 feel less like a novelty craft machine and more like a practical desktop tool.

The print-and-cut workflow adds the missing piece. Instead of treating color as a separate step handled by another device, the M2 can print a design and then cut or engrave around it in the same workspace. That matters for stickers, labels, ornaments, tags, and small merch projects where alignment is usually the part that ruins the mood. When it works well, the process feels less fussy than juggling a printer, cutter, transfer sheet, and a pile of measurements.

That variety is the M2’s strongest argument. It is not just a laser engraver with color tacked onto the side, and it is not just a craft printer with a cutter attached. It sits somewhere between those categories, making it more flexible than a basic cutting machine but less intimidating than a full-workshop laser. The best use cases are the ones that combine its strengths: engraved texture, cut shapes, printed color, and materials that make sense for the workflow.

This could become the beginner laser to watch

The M2 matters because it reduces friction

Credit: Source: xTool

The xTool M2 isn’t the most powerful machine in xTool’s lineup, and it isn’t trying to be. Its real appeal is that it packages several maker workflows into a safer, more approachable desktop device at a price that does not immediately push beginners away. The enclosed design, camera positioning, automated setup tools, and swappable modules all point toward the same goal. xTool wants the M2 to feel less like shop equipment and more like a household creative tool.

Deals

Save on Maker & Creator Deals for Laser, Print, and Craft Gear

Explore discounts on maker and creator deals — shop savings on desktop laser cutters, print-and-cut workstations, CMYK inks, replacement modules, and air filtration units. Compare offers and bundles to find the best value for your next craft setup.
Deals Explore Maker & Creator Deals

That does not mean buyers should ignore the fine print. The most affordable bundle may not include every module, color printing works best on specific materials, and serious laser use still requires ventilation planning. An optional air purifier is available, and xTool recommends air filtration for indoor laser workflows. Those are normal tradeoffs, but they are still tradeoffs worth making clear.

Even so, the M2 looks like one of xTool’s most accessible launches yet. The combination of color printing, laser cutting, engraving, and camera-assisted setup gives it a broader creative range than a basic craft cutter, while the $599 starting price keeps it from feeling locked behind pro-maker pricing. For users who want to personalize gifts, make small batches of merch, experiment with leather and coasters, or finally try print-and-cut projects without juggling multiple devices, this could be the xTool machine that makes the category feel less intimidating.

xTool M2 Color Craft Laser
$549 $599 Save $50
Brand
xTool
Laser Power
10W diode base, 20W diode and 3W IR available
Working Area
16.7in x 12.5in (424mm x 317mm
Software
xTool Studio
Connectivity
USB/Wi-Fi
$549 at xTool
item.content:encoded:
xTool recently announced the M2 Color Craft Laser, a new desktop crafting machine that starts at $599 and combines laser engraving, laser cutting, and CMYK color printing in one enclosed unit. Considering that desktop laser machines with beginner-friendly software, camera alignment, and enclosed safety designs usually move out of casual-crafter territory quickly, it’s encouraging to see a price under $600. The M2 is clearly aimed at the person who has outgrown a vinyl cutter or basic craft printer, but doesn’t want to turn a spare room into a full production shop.

Engraved texture, cut shapes, printed color, and smart alignment are what make the M2 feel different from a basic craft cutter.

The pitch is simple, but the device itself is more interesting. xTool isn’t just selling another diode laser with a new shell and friendlier marketing. The M2 adds a CMYK inkjet module, dual cameras for visual positioning, automated material-handling features, and swappable tool modules that let it switch between printing, cutting, engraving, and IR marking. That makes it feel less like a single-purpose laser engraver and more like a compact craft workstation, especially for creators who make personalized goods, stickers, leather accessories, coasters, signs, tags, and small merch runs.

Disclaimer: xTool provided me with the M2, equipped with a 10W diode laser module and a CMYK printhead, but had no input on this article.

8 unique 3D prints that I actually use every day

These are the genuinely useful 3D prints that I use the most in my home.

2
By  Adam Conway

The big feature is color without leaving the machine

Print and cut makes the M2 feel different

The headline feature is the M2’s color print-and-cut workflow. xTool says the CMYK inkjet module can print directly on materials such as wood, paper, felt, and similar porous surfaces, then use the laser to cut or engrave the same project without moving it to another machine. That is the part that could make the M2 click with everyday creators. Anyone who has bounced between a printer, cutting machine, transfer process, and alignment jig knows how quickly a simple project turns into a desk full of almost-right results.

The M2’s color printing is not the same thing as UV printing. It is best suited for porous or coated materials where the ink can bond properly, while smooth surfaces like glass, metal, or ceramic may need extra prep. That does not make the feature less useful, but it does mean buyers should think of it as a craft-focused print-and-cut system rather than a universal color-on-anything machine.

That workflow also gives the M2 a more approachable identity than many desktop lasers. Laser engraving can be intimidating because the best results often depend on material tests, alignment tricks, masking, ventilation, and a pile of settings that don’t mean much to a beginner. Adding color printing gives users something more familiar to start with. A printed sticker, tag, or small sign is easier to understand than dialing in engraving depth on an unfamiliar blank.

Credit: Source: xTool

This is where xTool’s camera system matters. The M2 uses dual cameras to provide a real-time view of the work area, with automated positioning and material detection handled through its ACS system. In plain terms, the machine is designed to show users where the design will land before they commit to the job. That won’t remove the need for testing, especially with laser work, but it does lower the number of ways a beginner can ruin material before learning anything useful.

The price makes this a much bigger announcement

This undercuts expectations for color-crafting hardware

The $599 starting price is the most aggressive part of the announcement. xTool already sells more advanced machines, including the M1 Ultra and higher-end laser systems, so the M2 is not trying to replace the company’s full lineup. It feels more like an attempt to bring the most approachable parts of those machines into a lower-cost package. That’s a smart move because the casual craft market is full of people who want better results than a blade cutter can offer, but don’t want to start with a four-figure machine.

There are limits baked into that price, of course. The base configuration starts with a 10W diode module, while xTool also lists support for a 20W diode module, a 3W IR module, and an inkjet module, depending on the configuration. That means buyers will need to pay close attention to bundles before assuming every feature shown in promotional material comes in the cheapest box. The M2’s flexibility is a real selling point, but modular systems always carry the risk that the most exciting workflows require additional spending.

Even with that caveat, the entry price changes the conversation. A $599 enclosed machine with color printing, camera preview, and laser capability is positioned far closer to mainstream craft hardware than to traditional workshop gear. It also gives xTool a different answer to users who might otherwise compare a laser cutter against a Cricut-style cutting machine, a sublimation setup, or a small UV printer. The M2 doesn’t beat all of those devices at everything, but it could be easier to justify as a first serious maker tool.

Real projects are where the M2 starts making sense

Leather, stone, and print-and-cut show its range

The M2’s spec sheet is useful, but the machine becomes much easier to understand once it starts producing finished objects. Engraving PU leather shows the softer, more personal side of what a compact laser can do, especially for small accessories, tags, patches, and custom gifts. Rock coasters push it in a different direction, giving the laser a harder surface where contrast, positioning, and repeatability matter more. Those two examples alone make the M2 feel less like a novelty craft machine and more like a practical desktop tool.

The print-and-cut workflow adds the missing piece. Instead of treating color as a separate step handled by another device, the M2 can print a design and then cut or engrave around it in the same workspace. That matters for stickers, labels, ornaments, tags, and small merch projects where alignment is usually the part that ruins the mood. When it works well, the process feels less fussy than juggling a printer, cutter, transfer sheet, and a pile of measurements.

That variety is the M2’s strongest argument. It is not just a laser engraver with color tacked onto the side, and it is not just a craft printer with a cutter attached. It sits somewhere between those categories, making it more flexible than a basic cutting machine but less intimidating than a full-workshop laser. The best use cases are the ones that combine its strengths: engraved texture, cut shapes, printed color, and materials that make sense for the workflow.

This could become the beginner laser to watch

The M2 matters because it reduces friction

Credit: Source: xTool

item.title: Windows 11's new driver allow-list could break your old hardware, and Microsoft won't tell you what's on it
item.itunes:title: Windows 11's new driver allow-list could break your old hardware, and Microsoft won't tell you what's on it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For most people running mainstream hardware that is modern, well-vetted, and relatively bland, drivers are a simple affair. Windows installs them, or in some instances, they come with the software you use for that piece of hardware, but either way, they're an afterthought. For most people, the April 2026 update is mostly unremarkable, with one quiet exception buried under a heading about driver security: Microsoft is introducing a curated allow-list for kernel drivers in Windows 11. For the users that run a weird capture card, old audio equipment, or some sort of legacy peripheral, the change behind this list may render it unusable.

Windows 11's driver signature requirement is one of the best anti-consumer security features out there

One of the best security features of Windows 11 is also inherently anti-consumer.

18
By  Adam Conway

Microsoft's new driver list is curated by telemetry

Application-agnostic

This driver list isn't built the way whitelists are typically constructed. Instead of having vendors apply or consulting end users, Microsoft built it internally, using what it describes as "billions of driver load signals and real-world usage data" gathered across Windows 11 and Windows Server 2025 over the past two years. This means, in not so many words, telemetry.

This also means the allow-list is weighted toward drivers that show up frequently in active Windows installations and have a clean reputation. If your hardware uses a driver that's running on millions of PCs and hasn't been associated with security incidents, it almost certainly made the list. If it uses a driver that's deployed across a few hundred enthusiast machines, even a perfectly legitimate one, there's a chance that it has been left off of the whitelist.

This is a pretty significant change from the previous model. Until now, any driver signed under Microsoft's cross-signed root program loaded by default, regardless of whether Microsoft had reviewed it recently or whether the certificate was still active. The new policy flips that around: only drivers Microsoft has explicitly chosen to keep trusting, plus anything signed through the current Hardware Compatibility Program, get to load. Everything else is blocked.

For anything mainstream, this is practically invisible, and won't affect day-to-day usage. For uncommon hardware, though, this may introduce a new host of issues.

Microsoft is changing how drivers arrive via Windows Update

This will improve security for Windows users

3
By  Rahul Naskar

You can't check what's on the list

Tough to know if you're affected

Besides introducing the compatibility issues themselves, the first real issue is that there's actually no way to check what hardware is on the allow-list. There's no big spreadsheet to reference; you can inspect drivers on your system or read the CodeIntegrity event log to see what gets flagged during the rollout's evaluation phase, but neither tell you whether a driver Microsoft considers untrusted today will get a pass in the future.

There's also no published roadmap for the list itself. Microsoft has described the allow-list as a transitional measure, with language like "limited number" and "smooth transition" suggesting it's meant to shrink over time as vendors migrate to current certification paths. But there's no commitment about how long or how much notice is given.

How to install Windows 11 on almost any unsupported PC

Want Windows 11 but have an unsupported computer? Here's how to install Windows 11 even if your PC doesn't meet the minimum requirements.

26
By  João Carrasqueira

Most users probably won't be affected

Your hardware is likely very safe

Microsoft isn't making this change by flipping a switch overnight. The April update ships the new policy in evaluation mode first, meaning the Windows kernel monitors and audits driver loads without blocking anything. Enforcement only activates once a system has accumulated 100 hours of runtime across multiple boot sessions and has loaded no untrusted cross-signed drivers during that window. If any cross-signed driver does load, the system stays in evaluation mode indefinitely until that driver either gets removed or becomes trusted through another route.

That means users of niche hardware aren't going to wake up to a non-functional PC tomorrow, and there's a pretty significant window in which someone can act on the driver conflict. For big organizations that control their own UEFI keys, this update could've caused some serious issues, but Microsoft gave them an "out." The Application Control for Business policy type lets them authorize their own custom drivers for kernel use, allowing them to continue to use custom drivers critical for operation. This doesn't matter for individual users, but Microsoft has clearly considered that some important hardware out there could become a paperweight.

It's also pretty unlikely that this affects you, just based on the hardware you probably use. The cross-signed root program was deprecated in 2021, the certificates have all expired, and most actively maintained hardware has been certified through the current program for years. The drivers actually affected by this change are old, often unmaintained, and frequently tied to vendors that no longer exist, which means you have to be using something pretty odd to be affected by this change.

Linux gamers didn't do anything wrong, but they might pay for Windows piracy anyway

Pirates on Windows might make life harder for gamers on Linux.

43
By  Adam Conway

What to do if you are affected

You don't have a ton of options

If a driver on your system gets flagged during this evaluation phase, you have a handful of realistic options, and none of them involve getting Microsoft to add the driver to the allow-list, because that's really unlikely.

Deals

Deals and savings on PC gear for modern work setups

Browse discounts and offers on computers & work-setup essentials — save on replacement peripherals, capture devices, audio interfaces, docking hubs, keyboards, and webcams. Shop deals to modernize aging hardware without breaking the bank.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The first thing worth checking is whether the affected hardware has a current driver available. It's one that's signed through the Hardware Compatibility Program rather than the old cross-signed program. Vendors that are still active have generally already migrated to current certification, and it's surprisingly common to find that the driver installed on a system is years older than what the manufacturer is currently shipping. The vendor's support page and Windows Update's optional updates section are both worth a look. If a newer signed driver exists, installing it usually ends the problem entirely.

Where things get harder is when the vendor is gone, dormant, or has simply stopped releasing drivers. For some long-discontinued hardware, community-maintained driver projects exist, but these are uncommon and not a route most users are going to feel comfortable taking, especially for kernel-level components. More often, the practical answer is replacing the hardware with something current, which is an unsatisfying conclusion for gear that still works perfectly well, but it's where most affected users will end up.

Where things get harder is when the vendor is gone, dormant, or has simply stopped releasing drivers. For some long-discontinued hardware, community-maintained driver projects exist, but these are uncommon and not a route most users are going to feel comfortable taking, especially for kernel-level components. More often, the practical answer is replacing the hardware with something current, which is an unsatisfying conclusion for gear that still works perfectly well, but it's where most affected users will end up.

item.title: AMD's FSR is helping my 7-year-old Nvidia GPU crush Forza Horizon 6
item.itunes:title: AMD's FSR is helping my 7-year-old Nvidia GPU crush Forza Horizon 6
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Forza Horizon 6 runs well on a 7-year-old GTX 1660 Ti with FSR, hitting 60+ fps at 1080p/1440p.
  • AMD FSR 3.1.5 Quality smooths edges and raises framerate, making High preset the sweet spot.
  • Upscaling keeps older GPUs relevant; you don't need a $1,000 upgrade to enjoy modern AAA games.

When the first gameplay trailer for Forza Horizon 6 came out, it got the entire internet talking about its "lack of optimization." Apparently, the game's usage of frame generation in its trailer veered the entire conversation toward performance metrics and VRAM usage charts. Now that the game is out, in typical modern gaming fashion, anything short of flawless 4K performance with ray tracing maxed out tends to become grounds for outrage.

Sitting away from all that noise, however, was my partner's humble GTX 1660 Ti PC, powered by a Ryzen 5 3600X and 32GB DDR4 RAM. On paper, this PC sounds dangerously close to becoming "obsolete" in 2026, but in practice, Forza Horizon 6 runs shockingly well on it. Sure, it's not perfect, and it's certainly not going to have every ray-traced bell and whistle enabled, but the game does run well enough to remind me that modern upscaling is nothing short of miraculous.

I went back to native resolution for a week, but Nvidia's DLSS still made it feel like the wrong choice

Technically superior no longer means visually better

13
By  Samarveer Singh

Forza Horizon 6 isn't as impossible to run as the internet says

A seven-year-old GTX 1660 Ti still has enough gas left in the tank

Forza Horizon 6's requirements certainly jumped up from its predecessor. It hasn't even been a full fortnight since the game came out, but major online outlets have already begun comparing its requirements and optimization with its predecessor, which was arguably one of the best-optimized games of its generation. While it should be obvious that not every one of the nearly 5 million users who have bought the game so far will be running it at maxed out settings with ray tracing turned all the way up, you'd be surprised at just how many folks are ready to call Forza Horizon 6 "unoptimized" just because it doesn't run at a perfect 4K/120fps for everyone.

Of course, it's not going to run at the highest preset with ray tracing for everyone, but for my partner who was excited about trying out the game on her own PC and Steam account, Forza Horizon 6 runs just fine. On a GTX 1660 Ti-powered PC with a Ryzen 5 3600X, Forza Horizon works incredibly well at both 1080p and 1440p, and for that, there's one singular feature we have to thank — AMD's FSR.

3 reasons I’d rather lower graphics settings than upgrade my GPU

Why spend thousands for modest gains?

11
By  Hamlin Rozario

AMD's FSR is better than TAA in Forza Horizon 6

No amount of gorgeous cars can make you ignore jagged edges

At this point, the GeForce 1660 Ti is seven years old, having come out in 2019, right before RTX took over Nvidia's GPU lineup completely. With 6GB of VRAM, it's barely above the minimum requirements of Forza Horizon 6, which asks for a GTX 1650 for a 1080p/60fps experience. Of course, running at a native 1080p resolution with a Ryzen 5 3600X, which is also seven years old, Forza Horizon 6 did manage to cross the 60 fps threshold at medium settings, which was pretty much what I was aiming for. Now, ray tracing simply was not on the table, even though the graphics card does boast ray-tracing capabilities, which Nvidia had retroactively added to it. However, the GTX 1660 Ti does not support DLSS, owing to its lack of Tensor cores required for upscaling.

GeForce GTX 1660 Ti + Ryzen 5 3600 | 1080p

Graphics Preset

Medium

High

Ultra

Native TAA

86 fps

63 fps

45 fps

FSR 3.1.5 Quality

91 fps

67 fps

54 fps

Thankfully, AMD's FSR 3.1.5 came to save the day here, with its Quality preset pushing the game comfortably into the 70+ fps territory at the High graphics preset. Even on native 1080p with TAA, the game managed to deliver 70–75 fps, but it was ghastly to look at. Jagged edges and shimmery foliage refused to let me focus on the road and the vehicle, and AMD's FSR Quality preset finally fixed things and smoothed those edges out. The game looked remarkable at 1080p High, delivering around 70 fps with occasional stutters below 60 fps. With AMD's FSR 3.1.5 upscaler, though, I got smoother edges and an even smoother framerate that jumped to the low 70s.

AMD's answer to DLSS arrived half-baked, and now nobody's using FSR Redstone

FSR Redstone's second showing has been... unimpressive

13
By  Samarveer Singh

At 1440p, the 1660 Ti needed FSR to cross the 60fps mark

Nothing wrong with helping the other team

Plugging the GeForce GTX 1660 Ti into a 1440p 144Hz display, I expected framerates in the low 60s with the Medium and High presets. Sadly, I only got as far as 64 fps on the Medium preset, with 1% lows in the 56–58 fps territory. This was at native 1440p resolution, which meant that jumping to high would be impossible without AMD's FSR 3.1.5 once again coming to the rescue. There's a world of difference in the Medium and High preset's model clarity, and the global illumination improves significantly, meaning that one must use the High preset if they can, somehow. At the high preset, it was FSR that got the game to an average of 65 fps, but the occasional stutter still dropped to 58 fps.

GeForce GTX 1660 Ti + Ryzen 5 3600 | 1440p

Graphics Preset

Medium

High

Ultra

Native TAA

69 fps

51 fps

34 fps

FSR 3.1.5 Quality

75 fps

65 fps

45 fps

Sadly, the game and the GPU were both going strong until I decided to check out the Ultra preset, which is where Forza Horizon 6 told me I was running out of VRAM. With the ultra textures loaded, even FSR couldn't help the game from dropping to 45–50 fps, with plenty of stutters here and there to break the immersion. At Ultra, the GTX 1660 Ti couldn't push anything past 35 fps, and even FSR Balanced struggled to get the game past the 45 fps mark. It was at this point that I decided to stick with 1440p High with FSR 3.1.5 Quality, marking it as the sweet spot on the latest AAA release in the industry.

I play at 4K on my 1440p monitor with DLSS 4.5, and I'm never going back

The image quality gains far outweigh the negligible performance dip

37
By  Samarveer Singh

Forza Horizon 6 looks gorgeous even on a 7-year-old GPU

The game's soul isn't affected by its graphics presets

Look, it's easy to notice and talk about the compromises when you start pixel-peeping. Using a preset like High instead of Ultra or Extreme gives you lower foliage density, and even the road textures lose their sharpness. When you flip your car over, the undersides look like they are just 240p textures stretched out, but those are compromises that are just as easy to ignore as they are to notice. In fact, look a little closer at the screenshots, and you'll see where the upscaler leaves clear ghosting trails around the cars' bodies. My partner isn't getting the same stunning ray-traced reflection bouncing across slick body panels that I am, nor is she experiencing the dramatic lighting interactions that make Forza Horizon 6 look so photorealistic at times.

Subscribe to our newsletter for practical GPU coverage

Explore more hands-on coverage of upscaling, optimization, and older GPU performance by subscribing to the newsletter. Expect focused analysis that clarifies settings trade-offs, real-world results, and practical takeaways.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

However, the soul of Forza Horizon 6 survives the downgrade intact. Drifting through a sakura-lined mountain curve at sunset still feels magical, even on a seven-year-old GTX 1660 Ti. The sense of speed, the atmosphere, the lighting, the overall presentation, and, of course, the beautiful playlist don't disappear when you downgrade to a lower graphics preset, despite the visual cuts. My partner never once felt like she was playing a "lesser" version of the game while we cruised online together, and that's the best part in all of this. Playground Games recommended an RTX 3060 for 1440p/60fps, yet the GTX 1660 Ti manages to get there comfortably with FSR 3.1.5 on the Quality preset.

Forza Horizon 6
Racing
Open-World
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 91/100 Critics Rec: 100%
Released
May 19, 2026
ESRB
Everyone / Mild Lyrics, Users Interact, In-Game Purchases
Developer(s)
Playground Games
Publisher(s)
Xbox Game Studios
14 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Genre(s)
Racing, Open-World
Powered by
I disabled frame generation in three AAA titles, and it proved some games are better off without it

Turns out, frame generation isn't the "magic fix" it's ought to be

4
By  Abhinav Raj

Upscaling isn't a crutch — it’s the reason older GPUs still matter

Not everyone can afford to swap graphics cards every two or three years.

At this point, I genuinely struggle to see upscaling as some kind of "fake frames" shortcut or crutch. Regardless of whether it's AMD's FSR or Nvidia's DLSS, these technologies are keeping older hardware relevant at a time when GPU prices continue to spiral into ludicrous territory with no signs of stopping. Not everyone can afford to swap graphics cards every two or three years, especially when mid-range upgrades now cost what entire gaming PCs used to.

That's why the GTX 1660 Ti running Forza Horizon 6 at 1440p High with FSR feels significant to me. It's proof that modern games don't automatically become inaccessible the moment you fall behind the hardware curve. Of course, there are going to be compromises, but smart upscaling lets players hold onto the experience itself without demanding a thousand-dollar entry fee every generation.

item.title: A $40 streaming stick fixed everything wrong with my smart TV
item.itunes:title: A $40 streaming stick fixed everything wrong with my smart TV
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Since I'm the entertainment editor here at XDA, I spend a lot of time testing different streaming sticks, set-top boxes, and of course, smart TV operating systems. Of all the devices I've used over the last few months, my mid-range 55-inch Hisense U8H's Google TV OS is by far the most frustrating. At times, I'm truly amazed at how bad it is.

Its Google TV operating system often lags like crazy due to caching issues, apps frequently fail to open, and sometimes the TV drops its internet connection entirely. To put it lightly, the experience isn't great. This is why I spend most of my time using a $180 Apple TV 4K (2nd-gen) from 2021. For the purposes of this story, though, I hooked the far cheaper $40 Fire TV Stick 4K Max (it's often on sale for $40) to see if a cheaper streaming device could just as easily replace my smart TV's shoddy Google TV operating system.

While the Fire TV Stick 4K Max isn't perfect, for the price, it's a great streaming device that fixes nearly everything wrong with my smart TV.

It might not be as flashy as the Apple TV 4K, but the Fire TV Stick 4K Max gets the job done

It's undeniably way better than my TV's stock Google TV operating system

The first thing I noted about the Fire TV Stick 4K Max is how responsive it is. Whether I'm navigating Netflix's convoluted menu system, avoiding Fire OS' never-ending stream of ads (which are undeniably frustrating — it seems Jeff Bezos has no chill), or streaming content, each click of the streaming stick's remote is responded to swiftly. With my TV's Google TV operating system, it felt like I needed to clear its cache nearly once a week for it to feel this snappy. Would I prefer that Amazon cut back a bit on the ads? You bet, but for $40, you get what you pay for, is sort of how I look at it.

Speaking of remotes, I've generally found the Fire TV Stick 4K Max's remote to be more reliable than my Google TV's. Every time I pick it up, it just works, whereas with my Hisense Google TV, I sometimes need to pop the batteries in/out to get it to connect properly to my TV. At first, I thought the issue was due to the remote's batteries being almost dead, but even after a fresh battery swap, I still encountered the problem. With the Fire TV Stick 4K Max's admittedly cheap-feeling remote, this hasn't been an issue.

There are other benefits the Fire TV's remote offers, too. If you're ingrained in Amazon's Alexa ecosystem, you'll likely prefer the Fire TV Stick's voice-activated assistant over Gemini/Google Assistant. You can even use your Fire TV Stick 4K Max remote to control your smart home devices.

While this isn't a feature I take advantage of very often, since my smart home is built around Google Home, making it better suited to Gemini/Google Assistant, I found myself sometimes controlling the Hue lights in my living room with Alexa. Surprisingly, they responded a bit faster than when I did the same thing with my Google TV-powered Hisense TV or nearby Nest Audio smart speakers. Whether this feature will be useful to you depends on if you already have Alexa/Echo-compatible devices in your home.

There are even cheaper Fire TV Stick options, too

If you want to sideload apps, make sure you avoid Vega OS-powered Fire TV Sticks

You don't need to go all in and buy the Fire TV Stick 4K Max, especially if you're just trying to modernize an older TV that's starting to show its age. Amazon offers several other cheaper Fire TV Sticks, including the $50 Fire TV Stick 4K (it's often on sale for between $34-$35), as well as its streaming sticks that have switched to Vega OS, including the $35 Fire TV Stick HD and the $50 Fire TV Stick Select. While the switch to Vega OS has been controversial, it's actually a decent, surprisingly snappy operating system, especially now that more third-party streaming apps are available.

That said, if you're interested in sideloading Android apps on your Fire TV Stick, you can't do so with Vega OS. While Amazon has repeatedly said that it released Vega OS to offer a lightweight alternative to its Android-powered Fire OS, the shift probably has more to do with how easy it is to install piracy-focused apps on the former operating system. It's likely that, as Amazon continues to refresh its various streaming sticks with new hardware, we'll see Vega OS slowly eclipse Fire OS, though that will take several years.

With that in mind, if you're interested in taking advantage of the Fire TV Stick's more open nature, you'll definitely want to pick one that supports Fire OS. In my case, since my Hisense TV is already powered by Google TV, I can sideload apps on my television with ease, though I'd rather use my Fire TV Stick 4K Max for that since it allows me to avoid my televisions other issue. But if you own a webOS LG TV or a Tizen Samsung TV, you'll likely want to take advantage of this. For example, I installed Wolf Launcher on my Fire TV Stick 4K Max. It's a solid-looking, clean menu that removes all of Amazon's annoying ads.

If you're looking for a cheap way to avoid your TV's less-than-stellar OS, a Fire TV Stick gets the job done

Do I wish Fire OS featured far fewer ads? For sure. Could its remote feel at least a bit higher quality? Definitely. But for the price, the Fire TV Stick 4K Max is one of the best ways to get rid of your smart TV's laggy operating system, replacing it with a snappy user interface powered by a snappy, capable streaming stick.

Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K Max
$60 at Amazon $55 at Best Buy $61 at Walmart
